title: Bessara
subtitle: Egyptian green fava bean and herb dip
excerpt: Dried split fava beans cooked until soft, then blended with an abundance of fresh flat-leaf parsley and dill until vivid green, thick, and creamy. Served cold or at room temperature with olive oil and cumin. Distinctly Egyptian — the bright green colour from the herbs is what distinguishes it from the Moroccan bissara.
sourceNotes: Bessara is an ancient Egyptian dish still sold from street carts in Cairo. The volume of fresh herbs, particularly flat-leaf parsley and dill, gives it its characteristic vivid green colour and distinguishes it from the Moroccan bissara, which uses no fresh herbs. Both dishes share the dried fava bean base but arrive at completely different results.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. Thin with water when reheating.

content.text: The herbs are the defining ingredient. Use both flat-leaf parsley and dill in generous quantities — at least half a bunch of each. The blending of the cooked beans with raw herbs creates the bright green colour. Add the herbs at the end of blending, after the beans are smooth, so they do not get overheated and lose their colour.

The texture of bessara is thick and spreadable, not soupy. It is served cold or at room temperature, scooped with bread. The olive oil drizzled on top is essential. Serve with sliced tomato and raw onion alongside if available.

items.prepNote: good quality, plus extra to serve

content.text: Cook, blend with herbs, and serve

Put the {{dried-split-fava-beans}} and {{garlic}} in a pot and cover with 1 litre of cold water. Bring to a boil, skim any foam, and simmer for 35 to 40 minutes until the beans are completely soft. Drain, reserving some cooking water. Transfer to a blender. Add the {{cumin-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{lemon-juice}}, and 3 tablespoons of the {{olive-oil}}. Blend until smooth. Add the {{parsley-flat}} and {{dill-fresh}} and blend again until vivid green. Add reserved cooking water a tablespoon at a time to reach a thick, spreadable consistency. Taste and adjust seasoning. Transfer to a serving plate. Drizzle with the remaining {{olive-oil}}, sprinkle with {{paprika-sweet}} and more cumin, and serve with bread.

items.symptom: Colour turning brown or grey instead of green
items.cause: Herbs blended with hot beans for too long
items.fix: Allow the beans to cool slightly (5 minutes) before blending. Add the herbs and blend quickly — the longer the herbs are blended, the more colour they lose

items.symptom: Dish tastes too similar to Moroccan bissara
items.cause: Herbs not used in sufficient quantity
items.fix: The herbs are what differentiate bessara. Use a full bunch of parsley and half a bunch of dill — more than seems necessary. The flavour should be herbaceous, fresh, and green

items.cause: Too much water added or beans very starchy
items.fix: Add water cautiously, a tablespoon at a time. Better to be too thick (easily corrected) than too thin (harder to reverse)

content.text: Bessara has been sold on Cairo streets since the pharaonic period — fava beans are one of the oldest cultivated crops in Egypt, and the tradition of cooking them into a paste dates back millennia. Today it is breakfast food, eaten with bread and raw vegetables at the side of the road. The vivid green colour, from the herbs blended in, sets it apart from its Moroccan cousin bissara (which is beige) and makes it instantly recognisable. Outside Egypt it is little known, one of the less familiar dishes in this collection.
